title: 2025/10/15 - EW
displayTitle: 2025-10-15 - Education and Workforce Committee - Petition of Sophie Liu: Change the Parent Resident Visa Expression of Interest (EOI) policy
topic: Petition of Sophie Liu: Change the Parent Resident Visa Expression of Interest (EOI) policy
description: Wednesday 19 February 2025 | Education and Workforce Committee Petition of Sophie Liu: Change the Parent Resident Visa Expression of Interest (EOI) policy Sophie Liu
descriptionSummary: Petition of Sophie Liu: Change the Parent Resident Visa Expression of Interest (EOI) policy. Sophie Liu

chunks.text: With you this afternoon on your petition. Um, so we'll give you an opportunity to introduce it and then if you'll allow us some time for questions, uh, that'd be appreciated. Over to you. Thank you. Hello everyone. My name is the petitioner. Thank you for your invite and being here to hear my oral sub mission today.

I assume you all have read my written sub mission carefully and watched the video of the Minister of Immigration, Eric Stanford, talking about the parent resident visa EOI issue and my petition. So today I won't repeat the supporting evidence and the data I already provided, but only highlight the key points to leave time for q and a.

First, I would like to highlight this issue at the broader country level, the CHE long-term fiscal statement 2025 and the stated that migration has delayed the agent pressures a unsaid hero article analyzed that treasure's projections rely on another million new immigrants arriving in New Zealand for the next 25 years to push the population up to 6.7 million.

Then the immigration master stay high to get the population to 7.4 million by 2065 to keep debt down to only 200% of GDP. This should be clear evidence that migrants are very important to the New Zealand economy. I would like you all to think about two questions.

One, how are you going to make New Zealand more attractive to compete with other countries in the world? Two, what kind of migrants do you want to attract and retain for New Zealand? Second, I would like to highlight this issue at the individual level.

The negative impacts on these sponsors, which were ignored when the policy was changed in 2022. Those sponsors are highly skilled with high incomes feeling critical skill shortage, paying significant taxes. I think this group of sponsors are exactly the migrants you would like to attract and retain.

However, our wellbeing is neglected because the current EOI setting are not reasonable and it's not fit for purpose, especially the balance system made it inaccessible, which is mentally torturing us. Some of the sponsors have already left in certain because they were too desperate to wait any longer with more sponsors are planning to leave.

If no improvements are made to the policy soon, I don't think you would like to see us leaving New Zealand, leaving New Zealand to go to Australia, our home countries, which will drive the New Zealand branching. Further in the MBI document, which listed the benefits that the parent category offers to New Zealand.

Item 43 stated for some migrants the potential to sponsor parents influenced their decision to immigrate to New Zealand and they were unlikely to have come here if their parents had not been able to join them. You must have read my personal story and how most sponsors who ated the EIS were misled by the Immigration New Zealand information missed out on the old queue,

which a hundred percent would have been selected by November, 2023, almost two years ago. Now we are still suffering in the ballot, so we respectfully request the Minister of Immigration to instruct Immigration New Zealand to make an extra selection immediately for all the ated UIs, approximately 2000 as a remedy and give us the wasted 2,708 quotas back.

Third, I would like to highlight this issue at applicant's level. They raised the sponsors whom have been contributing enormous like to New Zealand and living far away from them. They wanted to live with the sponsors together, help to look after their grandchildren, provide physical and emotional support.

More than 70% of the applicants age under 70, they were needed to pass medical examinations before the resident visas get approved anyway. So to ensure they are healthy. If you allow them to come to live in New Zealand as soon as possible, they are still healthy and young enough to contribute to the New Zealand society directly and indirectly as they get older.

They need to be more dependent on sponsors to live with dignity and the love in a supportive family environment. Unlike other resident category with Bella system like the Pacific access category or Samoa quota, our parents cannot try luck with another country like Australia because their children live in New Zealand.

This reunification is not just nice to have, it's a basic human need. It's our fundamental value and responsibility to give back to our parents and look after them, take care of them, especially for those who are only child. We wanna change. We urge the Minister of Immigration to start the review of the re current resident visa policy settings immediately rather than later off the year and into next year with the law proposed solution.

One, remove the two year expiry. If you must make the EIS expire every two years, then please illustrate waive the reapplication fee. Two, change the balance system back to the Q system and select EOIs based on the original date of sub mission for more certainty failed and transparency.

If you decided to keep the balance, then please let select more than 90% of EOIs based on the order of original date of sub mission each time. Then run a bonus draw for less than 10% from the unselected remaining UIs in the same pool. Three increased annual cap back to 5,500 visas on behalf of the one 15,427 sponsors in the Bay.

By end of July, I want you to truly say us, this group of people who have been suffering, hear us, feel us, care about us, save us from the pain. Use your authority, power, and inference to help us solve the problem. Now the government made a commitment to review the settings, but it has been delayed.

We are still waiting. As the chairperson of the committee, Kyle, can you please help us to make the commitment fulfilled? Thank you from the bottom of my heart. Thank you very much. Um, Sophie, there's a few questions I'm gonna start with Grant McCullum.

Yeah. Um, thank, thank you very much for your, um, your, your presentation petition obviously went a lot of effort. That's, uh, great work. Uh, just curious, so when the previous Labor League government decided to change a ballot process in 2023, do you know if any consultation was carried out with the migrant communities at The time? Based on my research, there was not.

There wasn't. Okay. Um, so have you had the opportunity to find out why there was no consultation? I think at that time, reading all the MBIE documents, there was so much call, well, I'm not the first one making petition about the category. Then we made a decision to reopen it.

It was back in July, 2000, uh, 22, and then announced that the change in the start selection in October, 2022, our first selection, November, 2022 for the queue. So I believe at that time to push it out there was for the time saving or something, there was no consultation being conducted.

Okay, thank you. Helen. What I, this is a big issue in my electorate in Mount Albert and I've heard from constituents about it even this week. So I I just wanted to say I, I do hear you and I do hear that there's a lot of torture in what's going on at the moment, the communities.

Hmm. What I wanna know is whether you've had discussions with the minister and what she has said she is doing about this at this point. At the, she acknowledged that this is a problem. Also the video which I linked on from YouTube, she promised that she will start the review of the parent resident visa after she completes the skilled micro category and the change of the skilled micro category was announced on 23rd of September.

I reached out to her again yesterday to invite her to attend the oral submission. She refuse and she said it's not appropriate for her to be here. And I asked her to confirm if the review of parent resident visa has been started yet, well should I say her Spokeperson or of official said Because the skilled migrant category work was also take longer than she expected, which at on 1st of July she said it would, it would take her a month or so.

So which count from that date now it's more than three months. And she said it has taken even longer. So she said not yet. And she said we'll start shortly, but I don't know when. That's the thing. I think the timeline keep changing. Yes. Even in my recent submission stuff, journalist reached out to her and her office March of this year, already said at that time they were doing the review but wasn't.

And then later, after the 1st of July, the conversation in person, her announcements was, it will be later this year, which now coming to the end of the year, I hope it's not getting delayed again because really it's so many people suffering. Mm-hmm.

Yeah. Got time for very quick final question. Pji. Yeah. So this is about the two year expiry on applications. Uh, so because people's circumstances can change while they're in the, in the system, you know, whether it's ballot at the moment, it's ballot system. So what do you think we can do to ensure, because sometimes people when they're in the system, they may not bother to pull out even if their circumstances have changed and that will, uh, waste a lot of time if they are drawn and they are, their application is processed

and then they realize actually they don't need it. So quick answer to deal with that. I think it's clear they will get declined if they get selected. I think first come first served for the people who is waiting. No, no, that was not my question. My question was the waste of time. So if they're being declined, that means somebody has handled their application and the time has been wasted.

Yeah. So it's when people are in the queue without that expiring period, people may not bother to come back and kind of informed that they do want to be in the system for consideration. How do we handle that? Okay, so I get what your question, even with the two years expire, that doesn't solve the problem because people still don't withdraw on time.

You cannot just wait for a expire. But also I think we should communicate better to tell people to withdraw proactively. That's what the principle runs in New Zealand based on trust. And also it should be for people doing that proactively. Rather say what all you can have a punishment, for example, your penalty attached with it says if you are actually no longer eligible but you don't withdraw waste other tax payers money or our our money, then maybe you can just have that thank you to make people be more responsible for the,

I think that, um, I think that brings our time, uh, to an end. Appreciate you taking the time to submit. Thank you very much. Your petition. Thank you. Thank you all. We'll report to the health new course, I'm sure. Thank you. Thank you.
